subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Two Particles — MVT, Trapezoidal Sum & Distance (Table)
stimulus:
The velocity of a particle $P$ moving along the $x$-axis is given by the differentiable function $v_P$, where $v_P(t)$ is measured in meters per hour and $t$ is measured in hours. Selected values of $v_P(t)$ are shown in the table. Particle $P$ is at the origin at time $t = 0$.

| $t$ (hours) | 0 | 0.3 | 1.7 | 2.8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $v_P(t)$ (meters per hour) | 0 | 55 | 29 | 55 |

parts.prompt: Justify why there must be at least one time $t$, for $0.3 \le t \le 2.8$, at which $v_P'(t)$, the acceleration of particle $P$, equals $0$ meters per hour per hour.
parts.scoring_notes: Alternate justification using Extreme Value Theorem: $v_P(0.3) = 55 > 29 = v_P(1.7)$ and $v_P(1.7) = 29 < 55 = v_P(2.8)$, so $v_P$ has a minimum in $(0.3, 2.8)$; since $v_P$ is differentiable, $v_P' = 0$ at that minimum.

scoring_points.description: Since $v_P$ is differentiable (hence continuous) on $[0.3, 2.8]$, by the Mean Value Theorem there exists $c \in (0.3, 2.8)$ such that $v_P'(c) = 0$

required_elements: Since $v_P$ is differentiable (hence continuous) on $[0.3, 2.8]$, by the Mean Value Theorem there exists $c \in (0.3, 2.8)$ such that $v_P'(c) = 0$

parts.prompt: Use a trapezoidal sum with the three subintervals $[0, 0.3]$, $[0.3, 1.7]$, and $[1.7, 2.8]$ to approximate the value of $\int_0^{2.8} v_P(t)\, dt$.
parts.scoring_notes: $0.3\left(\frac{0+55}{2}\right) + 1.4\left(\frac{55+29}{2}\right) + 1.1\left(\frac{29+55}{2}\right) = 8.25 + 58.8 + 46.2 = 113.25$ meters. Units not required for this point.

parts.prompt: A second particle, $Q$, also moves along the $x$-axis so that its velocity for $0 \le t \le 4$ is given by $v_Q(t) = 45t\cos(0.063t^2)$ meters per hour. Find the time interval during which the velocity of particle $Q$ is at least 60 meters per hour. Find the distance traveled by particle $Q$ during the interval when the velocity of particle $Q$ is at least 60 meters per hour.
parts.scoring_notes: Find $t$ values where $v_Q(t) = 60$: $A \approx 1.866$ and $B \approx 3.519$. Distance $= \int_A^B v_Q(t)\, dt \approx 106.109$ meters (velocity is positive throughout).

parts.prompt: At time $t = 0$, particle $Q$ is at position $x = -90$. Using the result from part (b) and the function $v_Q$ from part (c), approximate the distance between particles $P$ and $Q$ at time $t = 2.8$.
parts.scoring_notes: $x_P(2.8) \approx 0 + 113.25 = 113.25$ (using trapezoidal sum from part (b)). $x_Q(2.8) = -90 + \int_0^{2.8} v_Q(t)\, dt \approx -90 + 135.938 = 45.938$. Distance $\approx |45.938 - 113.25| \approx 67.312$ meters.
